one_line_takeaway: A CIA-triad-structured field catalog of the author's own real-world prompt injection exploits against every major LLM vendor, arguing the class has no deterministic fix and that untrusted-data-processing AI systems must be red-teamed under a 'Trust No AI' assumption.
key_finding: Prompt injection is not a single narrow bug but a cross-cutting failure that compromises all three CIA attributes across essentially every major commercial LLM vendor, and it currently has no deterministic fix. Confidentiality is broken by data exfiltration through image/link rendering and automatic tool invocation (a long list of vendors were vulnerable and patched); Integrity is broken by untrustworthy/tamperable output enabling phishing, conditional payloads, ASCII smuggling and terminal-escape attacks; Availability, the least-discussed attribute, is broken by recursive tool loops, forced refusals, and persistent memory-poisoning DoS. The recurring takeaway is 'Trust No AI': applications processing untrusted data must be threat-modeled, red-teamed, and given explicit security guarantees before release.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge / experience paper by Johann Rehberger (embracethered.com) that compiles the author's own publicly disclosed, real-world prompt injection exploits and proof-of-concepts against production LLM systems, and organizes them along the CIA security triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). The contribution is not a new attack technique or algorithm but a structured catalog that maps concrete, vendor-confirmed vulnerabilities (in OpenAI ChatGPT, Microsoft Bing Chat / M365 Copilot / GitHub Copilot / Azure AI, Anthropic Claude, Google Bard/Gemini/Vertex/NotebookLM/AI Studio/Colab, Amazon Q, DeepSeek, Slack/Discord/Teams) onto each security attribute, together with the mitigations vendors applied. The author explicitly frames it as bridging the gap between academic and industry security research. Distinguishing own work from cited work: the paper credits Preamble with first discovering the vulnerability class (2022), Willison with naming 'prompt injection', Goodside with the invisible-Unicode-tag / malicious-input observations, Greshake et al. with formalizing indirect prompt injection, S. Roman with the original markdown-image exfiltration idea, and Derczynski with the observation that LLMs emit ANSI escape codes. The author's OWN contributions are the specific end-to-end exploits and tools built on top of those primitives: the 'ASCII Smuggler' tool, the M365 Copilot ASCII-smuggling data-exfiltration chain, SpAIware (persistent memory-based spyware/C2 in ChatGPT), ZombAIs (hijacking Claude Computer Use into a botnet zombie), conditional prompt injection in M365 Copilot, Terminal DiLLMa (ANSI-code exploitation of CLI LLM tools) plus its caret-notation mitigation, the DeepSeek XSS-to-account-takeover exploit, and persistent denial-of-service via poisoned ChatGPT memory.
threat_model: The adversary controls untrusted data that is commingled with trusted system/user instructions inside the final prompt sent to an LLM (the paper's core definition of prompt injection). The dominant vector is INDIRECT prompt injection: malicious instructions embedded in web pages, documents, images, emails, or tool outputs that an LLM-integrated application ingests during normal operation, with no privileged access required. Because system prompt and user input share the same trust boundary, and because there is no deterministic fix (contrasted explicitly with SQL injection), the attacker can override original instructions, exfiltrate data (via markdown image rendering, clickable/unfurled hyperlinks, or automatic tool invocation to third-party servers), tamper with LLM output (phishing/scam content, conditional payloads targeting specific users, invisible Unicode/ANSI characters), or degrade availability (recursive tool-call loops, forced refusals, persistent memory-poisoning DoS). Vendor-side mitigations discussed include url_safe / CSP restrictions on rendering untrusted domains, human-in-the-loop confirmation for consequential tool actions (e.g. OpenAI's x-is-consequential flag), disabling link unfurling, output encoding, and rate/loop limits.
limitations: The paper is a curated compilation of the author's own disclosures, so it is not a systematic or exhaustive survey and carries selection bias toward the systems the author personally tested. It offers no quantitative measurement of prevalence, severity, or exploit success rates, and no comparative or reproducible evaluation. Many mitigations are described qualitatively without effectiveness data, and the author himself notes prompt injection has no deterministic solution, so the mitigation list is best-effort risk reduction rather than a guaranteed defense. Several cited findings are attributable to other researchers, so care is needed not to conflate the field's discoveries with this author's contributions.

quantitative_results: Availability / infinite-loop scenario: when the author tested recursive tool invocation against ChatGPT, OpenAI had already anticipated the threat and ChatGPT permitted only 10 tool invocations in a loop (the sole numeric result in the paper).
